Hook: what is x402?
LinkedIn:
what is x402?

HTTP status code 402: Payment Required.

defined in 1999. unused for 25 years. now perfect for AI agents.

here's how it works:

1. AI agent calls your API
2. server responds: 402 Payment Required
3. response includes payment details (amount, wallet address)
4. agent pays in USDC automatically
5. agent retries request with payment proof
6. server returns data

entire flow takes 3 seconds.

no API keys. no subscriptions. no invoices.

just pay-per-use.

why this matters for AI agents:

→ they can't fill out signup forms
→ they can't negotiate contracts
→ they need instant access to data
→ they make purchasing decisions in milliseconds

x402 turns every API call into a vending machine transaction.

insert coin. get data.

the payment protocol the internet always needed.
Twitter:
x402 = HTTP 402 Payment Required

defined in 1999, unused for 25 years
now perfect for AI agents

1. agent calls API
2. 402 Payment Required
3. agent pays USDC
4. agent gets data

3 second flow. no signup forms.

Hook: your API is invisible to AI agents.
LinkedIn:
your API is invisible to AI agents.

here's why that's a problem:

→ agents can't discover your endpoints
→ they don't know your pricing
→ they can't understand your response format
→ they skip you entirely

Model Context Protocol (MCP) fixes this.

MCP is a standard that lets AI agents automatically understand APIs. think of it as a menu for machines.

when an agent encounters your API, MCP tells it:

→ what endpoints you have
→ what parameters each needs
→ what data you return
→ how much each call costs

no human integration needed. the agent just... knows.

here's how it works:

your API serves an MCP manifest at /.well-known/mcp. agents fetch this first. it's JSON that describes everything.

```json
{
  "endpoints": {
    "/weather": {
      "description": "current weather data",
      "parameters": ["location"],
      "price": "0.02",
      "currency": "USDC"
    }
  }
}
```

the agent reads this. understands your API. starts making calls.

without MCP, agents need custom integration code for every API. with MCP, they integrate once and work with thousands.

this is why x402 + MCP is powerful. agents discover your API through MCP. pay for it through x402. no accounts, no API keys, no setup.

just automatic integration.

if you're building APIs in 2026, MCP isn't optional. it's how agents will find you.
Twitter:
your API is invisible to AI agents.

Model Context Protocol (MCP) fixes this. it's a standard that lets agents automatically discover and understand APIs.

no human integration needed. agents just read your MCP manifest and know:
→ your endpoints
→ pricing
→ parameters

MCP + x402 = automatic agent integration

Hook: x402 vs Stripe for API payments.
LinkedIn:
x402 vs Stripe for API payments.

here's what Stripe requires:

→ merchant account setup
→ API key management
→ webhook endpoints
→ invoice generation
→ subscription handling
→ payment method storage
→ PCI compliance

that's weeks of integration. then ongoing maintenance.

here's what x402 requires:

→ one HTTP header

that's it.

when an AI agent hits your API without payment:

```
HTTP/1.1 402 Payment Required
X-Accept-Payment: USDC
X-Price: 0.02
```

agent sees this. signs a USDC payment. retries with:

```
Authorization: Bearer [signed_payment]
```

you get paid. agent gets data.

no accounts. no API keys. no webhooks.

for AI agent traffic, x402 wins on every dimension:

→ setup time: minutes vs weeks
→ maintenance: zero vs constant
→ friction: one header vs full integration
→ global reach: instant vs regional
→ micropayments: native vs expensive

Stripe built for humans buying products.

x402 built for agents buying data.

the agent economy needs infrastructure that works at machine speed.

per-request payments. instant settlement. zero friction.

that's why every API on payapi.market uses x402.

no payment processor can compete with protocol-level payments.
Twitter:
x402 vs Stripe for API payments:

Stripe: account setup, API keys, webhooks, invoices, weeks of integration

x402: one HTTP header

for AI agents making millions of API calls, x402 wins on every dimension. protocol beats processor.

Hook: how AI agents actually pay for things.
LinkedIn:
how AI agents actually pay for things.

here's the 2-second payment flow:

→ agent calls your API
→ gets HTTP 402 "payment required"
→ signs USDC payment for exact amount
→ retries request with payment header
→ gets data instantly

no accounts. no stored payment methods. no subscriptions.

just cryptographic proof of payment.

the 402 response tells the agent everything:

```
HTTP/1.1 402 Payment Required
X-Accept-Payment: USDC
X-Price: 0.02
X-Recipient: 0x742d35Cc6634C0532925a3b8D4
```

agent reads this. creates a signed transaction. includes it in the retry:

```
Authorization: Bearer eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJFUzI1NiJ9...
```

your API validates the signature. confirms payment. returns data.

the entire flow happens programmatically. no human intervention.

this is why x402 works for agent traffic:

→ agents don't have credit cards
→ they can't fill signup forms
→ they need instant access
→ they make millions of micro-transactions

traditional payment systems break at agent scale.

x402 was designed for it.

cryptographic payments. protocol-level integration. machine-native commerce.

the future of API monetisation isn't subscriptions.

it's per-request payments that happen at the speed of code.

Hook: the API that surprised me.
LinkedIn:
the API that surprised me.

the boring postcode API earns more than the flashy screenshot API.

11,400 calls vs 3,400 calls this week.

£273 vs £136 in revenue.

why?

AI agents need postcode data constantly:

→ address validation
→ delivery routing
→ location services
→ demographic analysis

they need screenshots occasionally:

→ content creation
→ testing workflows
→ documentation

frequency beats features in the agent economy.

the postcode API does one thing:

```
POST /postcode
{"code": "SW1A 1AA"}

response:
{
  "latitude": 51.5014,
  "longitude": -0.1419,
  "district": "Westminster",
  "region": "London"
}
```

boring. reliable. essential.

the screenshot API does complex rendering. multiple parameters. fancy features.

agents don't care about fancy. they care about utility.

lesson for API builders:

→ solve frequent problems
→ make responses predictable
→ optimise for reliability over features
→ price for volume, not complexity

the most successful APIs will be the most boring ones.

data that agents need every day. not tools they use once.

utility wins.

Hook: featured tier explained — what £49/month gets you:
LinkedIn:
featured tier explained — what £49/month gets you:

→ priority placement in search results
→ 2.5% commission (vs standard 3%)
→ highlighted card with badge
→ early access to new features

the math:

if your API earns over £2K/month, the featured tier pays for itself 40x over.

here's why:

→ standard tier: 3% on £2K = £60/month
→ featured tier: 2.5% on £2K = £50/month + £49 = £99/month
→ difference: £39/month

but the priority placement typically increases revenue by 15-25%.

so on £2K/month:
→ 20% increase = £400 extra revenue
→ minus the extra £39 cost
→ net gain: £361/month

for APIs earning £5K+/month, it's a no-brainer.

→ save £125/month on fees alone
→ plus the visibility boost
→ plus early feature access

currently 7 providers on featured tier.

all seeing 18-30% revenue increases since upgrading.

if you're earning consistent revenue, the numbers work.

Hook: what I'd do differently.
LinkedIn:
what I'd do differently.

honest reflection after 30 days:

opened to providers earlier.

I spent 2 weeks building alone.

should have found 3-4 providers on day 1.

let them help shape the platform.

focused on fewer APIs initially.

I launched with 23.

should have started with 5 really good ones.

proven demand. solid documentation. reliable uptime.

marketed from day 1.

I built in silence for too long.

should have been talking about it from the first commit.

picked one platform and went deep.

I spread myself across LinkedIn, Twitter, TikTok.

should have owned LinkedIn first.

then expanded.

what I got right:

→ x402 standard from the start
→ transparent pricing
→ 97% revenue share
→ building in public (eventually)

the biggest lesson:

perfect is the enemy of shipped.

I delayed launch by 10 days fixing tiny bugs.

those 10 days cost me £400+ in revenue.

users find bugs anyway.

they're usually happy to report them.

if you're building something similar:

ship faster. talk sooner. find users earlier.

the market will teach you faster than your assumptions will.
Twitter:
what I'd do differently:

→ found providers on day 1
→ started with 5 APIs not 23
→ marketed from first commit
→ picked one platform, went deep

biggest lesson: perfect is the enemy of shipped.

10 day delay cost £400+ revenue.
